topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of stale coffee and old paper suddenly felt suffocating. I found the evidence tucked beneath a loose floorboard in my room: pages from my diary, meticulously marked, interspersed with my own frantic scribbles. My stomach twisted into a tight knot. I watched my roommate, Mark, across the room, humming tunelessly while flipping through the pages, a faint, almost smug smile playing on his lips. A slow, cold hardening settled in my chest. I didn't speak. I simply picked up a heavy textbook, the sound of the cover hitting the desk echoing loudly in the sudden, charged silence. Every movement I made was deliberate, measured, aimed not at confrontation, but at a slow, precise dismantling of his comfort.

[story 2]

It started as a dull ache, a simmering resentment that had been building since move-in day. Then, the discovery—my private thoughts, laid bare on the desk, being casually absorbed by someone else. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles strain beneath my skin. I didn't yell. That would have been too messy, too uncontrolled. Instead, I began rearranging things. Books were stacked with aggressive precision, notes were alphabetized according to a scheme only I understood, and every shared space became a carefully constructed monument to my displeasure. When Mark finally looked up, his expression shifting from relaxed curiosity to something guarded, I met his gaze with an unnerving stillness. My voice, when it finally emerged, was low, smooth, and utterly devoid of warmth, like polished stone.

[story 3]

The realization hit me like a physical blow—that my most guarded secrets were being treated like casual reading material by the person I shared a room with. A hot flush crept up my neck, quickly replaced by a chilling resolve. I spent the next hour in a silent storm, my hands trembling slightly as I gathered every personal item—pens, photos, loose papers—and placed them into a sturdy, opaque box. The sound of the latch clicking shut was sharp and final. I didn't need to say anything. The way I packed, the way I avoided his eyes, the rigid set of my shoulders—it all communicated a deep, simmering dissatisfaction. I watched him try to engage, to offer an apology, but I remained an impenetrable wall of quiet fury, letting the silence between us do the heavy lifting.
